topic: Async - Send Bounds on Futures and Spawning Constraints

turns.content: I've read about async send bounds but I still don't get why my code keeps failing with that 'future cannot be sent between threads' error. I thought if I just made the function async it would automatically work when I tried to spawn it.

That's a common misconception. The problem is that an async fn isn't Send unless every single await point in its body holds values that are already Send. If you capture something like Rc, RefCell, or raw pointers across those await points, the future itself becomes !Send because it can't be safely moved between threads.
So I shouldn't use Rc at all? That sounds painful for my tooling. Is there a way to just wrap everything in an atomic ref cell or something?

AtomicRefCell won't solve this because it's still !Send by default. The real fix is either restructure your code so you don't hold non-Send values across await points, or use tokio Mutex instead of std Mutex if you need shared mutable state inside the future. You can also store the value before the await and pass only a reference after.

Ah okay, but my pipeline is failing because I'm trying to spawn multiple workers that all need to access the same config data which is currently in RefCell. How do I fix this without refactoring everything?

You should switch to tokio Mutex since it's Send and Sync, or move the configuration out of the future entirely into a separate shared state holder that workers access via channels instead of trying to share the RefCell directly. If you must keep it inside, remember that the guard type for std Mutex isn't Send so spawning will still fail unless you restructure.
That makes sense but I'm worried about deadlocks now. What's the best pattern for passing data between threads without blocking?

The cleanest approach is to avoid crossing await boundaries with non-Send types entirely. Use channels like tokio::sync::mpsc or shared ownership patterns where workers receive cloned references rather than holding mutable state across spawns. This avoids the Send constraint issue altogether.
Got it, so I need to refactor my pipeline to use tokio Mutex and pass data through channels instead of trying to share RefCell directly? And make sure all await points don't hold !Send values?

Exactly. Refactor your pipeline to use tokio Mutex for shared mutable state and ensure no await point holds non-Send types like raw pointers or plain RefCell guards. Then when you spawn workers, they'll get a Send future that can safely cross thread boundaries without the 'future cannot be sent between threads' panic.

topic: Async - Send Bounds on Futures and Spawning Constraints

turns.content: Hi, I'm working on an async Rust tool to automate some repetitive tasks. Should I use tokio or async-std?
Good question! For most cases, tokio is the de facto choice because it has better ecosystem support and more features like time-based scheduling. However, both are great; just pick one.
Okay, so I want to spawn tasks asynchronously. What pitfalls should I watch out for?

Good question! One common pitfall is forgetting that async functions in Rust are Send by default only if all awaited futures hold no !Send values. For example, Rc or RefCell can cause non-Send types inside your futures, which means you can't spawn those futures across threads with tokio::spawn.
So if I have a MutexGuard inside an async function and await on it, is that Send?
Good point! A MutexGuard itself isn't Send. But you can use tokio::Mutex or Arc<Mutex<T>> to wrap your data, which then implements Send safely.
What if I want to store a value before awaiting on it? Will that cause problems?
Yes, if the value you're storing is non-Send (like Rc<T> or RefCell), then you can't move it into an async closure and spawn it. Instead, clone or pass only Send-safe parts of your data around.
Oh wait—what if I just wrap the whole thing in a Mutex before spawning?
That's right! Wrapping the entire async closure or struct inside a tokio::Mutex ensures it becomes Send-safe, allowing you to spawn it across threads safely.
